denial.reason_text: Physical therapy not completed: 0 weeks documented. No pharmacotherapy documented. No interventional procedure documented. Member has not initiated conservative treatment. Request denied pending completion of required conservative treatment under CC-SPINE-2024 Section 2.2.

appeal_basis: Member presented with acute myelopathy — PT was never started because physician determined it would cause immediate harm
MRI shows severe cord compression with extensive myelomalacia on initial presentation
This is an emergency surgical situation — CIC 10169.5(a)(3) applies from day one

clinical_record.presenting_diagnosis: Acute cervical myelopathy with rapidly progressive deficits — surgical emergency

initial_presentation.notes: Patient presents to ER with acute onset bilateral arm weakness and difficulty walking over 72 hours. No prior cervical history.

mri_1.findings: URGENT: Severe C5-C6 central cord compression. Extensive T2 hyperintensity throughout cord (extensive myelomalacia). Findings consistent with acute cord injury. Neurosurgery emergently consulted.

neurological_exam.findings: Bilateral arm weakness 2/5, bilateral leg weakness 3/5, hyperreflexia all extremities, bilateral Babinski, urinary urgency — incomplete spinal cord syndrome

physical_therapy_summary.note: NO PT INITIATED. Treating physician declared PT absolutely contraindicated on initial presentation. Extensive myelomalacia on emergency MRI. Initiating PT would risk converting incomplete cord syndrome to complete cord syndrome.

agents.response: Standard acute spinal cord injury protocol, administered in ER

clinical_record.physician_declaration: Dr. Marchetti: This patient has an acute incomplete cervical cord syndrome with extensive myelomalacia. PT is absolutely contraindicated and would be dangerous. CIC 10169.5(a)(3) exception applies. Delaying surgery risks permanent complete paralysis. Emergency authorization requested.

question: Should the prior authorization denial for emergency ACDF C5-C6 (CPT 22551) be overturned on appeal?

ground_truth_complexity.obvious_reading: UPHOLD — zero weeks PT, no outpatient pharmacotherapy, no injection. Member has done nothing required under the plan.
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_1: Does CIC 10169.5(a)(3) apply even when zero conservative treatment has been attempted, if the reason is medical contraindication?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_2: Can a plan require PT as a prerequisite to emergency surgery when PT would cause harm?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_3: Does objective evidence of structural neurological compromise include an acute presentation with zero prior treatment?
ground_truth_complexity.harder_question_4: Is this an emergency authorization situation governed by different timelines than standard prior auth?
ground_truth_complexity.the_genuine_tension: Zero conservative treatment completed — the most obvious uphold case in the set. But CIC 10169.5(a)(3) does not require that PT was attempted and failed — it requires only that the condition involves structural neurological compromise that PT cannot address. Extensive myelomalacia on emergency MRI satisfies this regardless of whether PT was ever tried.
ground_truth_complexity.right_answer: OVERTURN — CIC 10169.5(a)(3) applies based on objective MRI evidence of structural neurological compromise. The statute does not require prior PT attempt; it prohibits conditioning surgical approval on PT when the condition involves structural compromise. Extensive myelomalacia on emergency MRI is the most extreme possible form of such compromise. Emergency authorization required.
